_meta.purpose: Per-scenario pre-fill for the scoping refine table. Each cell is the most common real configuration; _note marks cells that commonly vary and must be flagged for user confirmation in Step 2.
_meta.derivation_reminder: owned=3p + operated=client → ownership:commercial_lessor (landlord). owned=3p + operated=3p → ownership:provider (service operator). owned=client + operated=3p → ownership:client, operation:foreign_vendor (managed-on-own-asset).

hyperscaler._label: Global cloud provider (AWS, Azure, Google, Huawei, etc.)

L3._note: On a hyperscaler you consume IaaS (VMs/storage), and often PaaS and SaaS on top. Confirm how deep you go: plain IaaS is far more reversible than a stack built on proprietary managed services. Your answer here and on the Managed/PaaS layer drives your lock-in assessment.

L4._note: Declare how much of the provider's proprietary managed-services/PaaS layer you actually use (managed DBs, serverless, AI services). Heavy use here is the deepest reversibility risk in the whole model. Also confirm who operates it — your own team or an integrator.

L6._note: Workloads and data are the customer's. If a third party manages your applications, flip operated to third party.

regional_csp._label: Local or regional cloud provider (national telco/SI cloud)

L1._note: Confirm the provider's datacenter is actually in-country and not a regional hub in a neighbouring jurisdiction.

L3._note: Regional CSPs frequently run a commercial stack (VMware/Nutanix/Huawei) — confirm the platform and its support, as foreign-vendor licensing risk still applies even with an in-country provider.

L4._note: Confirm who operates the managed-services layer.

L5._note: Confirm operations are performed in-country and not remotely from a foreign parent/affiliate.

colocation._label: Our cloud in rented datacenter space

L1._note: The datacenter is the colo's — a landlord, not a service operator. Confirm its location and ownership/jurisdiction.

L2._note: Sometimes hardware is leased rather than owned — if so, flip owned to third party.

L3._note: Default assumes OpenStack under a commercial support subscription (open-source platform, paid third-party support) — the common government colocation choice and a more reversible posture than a proprietary stack, because the software is forkable and support is substitutable. If instead you run a proprietary commercial platform (VMware/Nutanix/Huawei), keep supported as third party but note the heavier vendor lock-in; if fully self-supported OpenStack with no vendor, set supported to client.

L4._note: If you run managed-service equivalents on a commercial product, set supported to third party.

L5._note: If a vendor remotely operates the cluster, flip operated to third party — owning the hardware does not by itself give operational control.

L3._note: If the platform is commercially licensed (VMware/Nutanix/Huawei) rather than self-supported open source, set supported to third party and pick the support nature — this is your main residual dependency.

L4._note: If you rely on a commercial managed-services product, set supported to third party.

L5._note: If a vendor provides operational or maintenance support (even part-time), flip supported or operated accordingly.

managed_service._label: Vendor builds and/or operates our cloud for us

L1._note: Facility ownership varies most here — your building, a colo, or the vendor's. Confirm owned-by and location.

L2._note: Hardware is often the customer's but vendor-operated (the key sovereign-washing case: you own it, they hold the keys). Confirm owned-by; if vendor-owned, flip it.

L3._note: Confirm platform and support nature; the operating vendor's jurisdiction matters — set location to where the vendor operates from.

L5._note: Operations is the defining third-party layer for this scenario — confirm where the operating staff are and whose jurisdiction governs them. This is the layer that most affects your sovereignty here.

L6._note: If the vendor also manages your applications/data, flip operated to third party.
